title: Number of Islands
problem_statement: Given an m x n 2D binary grid grid which represents a map of '1's (land) and '0's (water), return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and is formed by connecting adjacent lands horizontally or vertically. You may assume all four edges of the grid are all surrounded by water.
intuition: Each connected component of '1's (land) forms an island. We can count the number of such components using traversal algorithms.

approaches.name: DFS Traversal
approaches.description: Iterate through the grid. When finding a '1', increment island count and DFS to mark all connected '1's as visited (change to '0').
approaches.space: O(m * n) worst case recursion stack
approaches.why_not: BFS is iterative and avoids stack overflow; Union-Find is overkill for grids.
interview_commentary: This is a graph traversal problem where each cell is a node, connected to up/down/left/right neighbors.
I'll iterate through each cell. When I find a '1', it's the start of a new island, so increment count and flood-fill to mark all connected land as visited.
Changing '1' to '0' in-place saves space for a visited array.

approaches.name: BFS Traversal
approaches.description: Same as DFS but use a queue for BFS. When finding a '1', enqueue and explore level by level, marking visited.
approaches.why_not: DFS is recursive; BFS is iterative and better for large grids.
interview_commentary: BFS is similar to DFS but uses a queue to explore level by level.
This avoids recursion stack issues, which is good for large grids (300x300).
Same time complexity, but space is better as queue size is bounded by grid width.

approaches.name: Union-Find
approaches.description: Treat each land cell as a set. Union adjacent lands. Count the number of distinct roots for land cells.
approaches.why_not: Overkill for this problem; traversal is simpler and more intuitive.
interview_commentary: Union-Find can count connected components. Each land cell is a node, union with right and down neighbors if land.
Then, collect all unique roots for land cells to get the count.
This is useful if you need to handle dynamic unions, but for static grids, traversal is easier.

interview_qa.q: Why do we modify the grid in-place?
interview_qa.a: To mark visited cells without extra space. Since the problem doesn't require the original grid, this is efficient.

interview_qa.q: What if the grid has no land?
interview_qa.a: Return 0, as there are no islands.

interview_qa.q: How does this differ from connected components in a graph?
interview_qa.a: It's the same: counting connected components where edges are between adjacent lands.
No, only horizontal and vertical as per the problem.
